english: col.; rdoi (rdo'i) of stone, rdoi túb - pa (rdo'i thub pa) a stone Buddha Glr.; rdo skyéd - pa (rdo skyed pa), skyá - ba (skya ba), to carry or drag stones to a place; *do-čág čóg-pa* (*do cag cog pa*) C. a ceremony observed in making a contract, by breaking a stone and using the fractured side as a seal, cf. mdzúg-gu ○túd-pa (mdzug gu gtud pa); rdo-bčál btín-ba (rdo bcal btin ba) Sch.: 'stones arranged according to their species'; *do-rúb-la táṅ-če* (*do rub la tang ce*), *do-rúb táṅ-te sád-če* (*do rub tang te sad ce*) W. to pelt, beat, or kill with stones, to stone; rdo rus tug (rdo rus thug) to the last extremity Sch.; dṅúl - rdo (dngul rdo) a stone containing silver, silver-ore Lex.; sprin-rdo (sprin rdo) a sort of marble Cs.; sbrá - rdo (sbra rdo) Sch., (perh. spra-rdo? (spra rdo?)) asbestus; mé - rdo (me rdo) fire-stone, flint; rmaṅ-rdo (rmang rdo) foundation-stone; zúr-rdo (zur rdo) corner-stone; gsér-rdo (gser rdo) a stone containing gold, gold-ore Cs. Comp. rdo-klád (rdo klad) a stone resembling a sheep s brain, and used as a remedy for diseases of the brain S.g. — rdo dkár (rdo dkar) Cs. a white stone; Sch. alabaster. — rdo-skrán (rdo skran) a kind of steatite or soap-stone. — rdo-ká (rdo ka) a vein in a stone. — rdo-kóg (rdo kog) a stone pot. — rdo-mkrís (rdo mkris) gall-stone(?) S.g. — rdo-rgyúd (rdo rgyud) various kinds of soft stone, as serpentine, soap-stone, chalk. — rdo - rgyús (rdo rgyus) S.g.? *do-čág* (*do cag*) C. oath taken in the above mentioned ceremony. — rdo-čál (rdo cal) Sik. = rdo-gżál (rdo gzhal). — rdo-čár (rdo char) a shower of stones; hail Schr. — rdo-○čáṅ (rdo 'cang), W. *dom-čáṅ* (*dom cang*), a stone of such a size as may be grasped by the hand. — rdo-mnyen (rdo mnyen) Cs. = ka-ma-ru (ka ma ru) a soft kind of stone, alabaster. — rdo-snyíṅ (rdo snying) jasper Sch. — rdo-tál (rdo thal) Cs. stone - ashes, calcined stone; Sch. quicklime, Schf. Tar. 103, 14: chalk; rdo-tál byúgs-pa (rdo thal byugs pa) to rough-cast, to plaster. — rdo-drég (rdo dreg) S.g.? Sch. dirt on stones. — rdo-snúm (rdo snum) rock-oil, petroleum Schr. — *do-pé* (*do pe*) W. stone-dust, small particles or grains of stone. — rdo bóṅ-ba (rdo bong ba) a shining black stone Cs. — rdó-bos (rdo bos) (perh. do-bos (do bos)) a large hammer, mallet Ld. — do - dbyúg (do dbyug) a sling-stone S.g. — rdo - ○bum (rdo 'bum) a sacred heap of stones, a mani. — rdo-sbóm (rdo sbom) large, heavy stones Sch. — rdo-rtsíg (rdo rtsig) stone-wall — rdo-tsád (rdo tsad) (= yám-bu (yam bu), rta-rmíg-ma (rta rmig ma) Cs.) a bar of silver-bullion, of about 156½ tolas (4 pounds) in weight, the common medium of barter in Central Asia. — rdo-żun (rdo zhun) Lt. = brag-żun (brag zhun) bitumen, mineral pitch (?) — rdo-żo (rdo zho) lime, both quick lime and slaked lime C. — rdo-gżál (rdo gzhal) a stone-pavement. — rdo-gżógs (rdo gzhogs) a cut or wrought stone Cs. — rdo-zám (rdo zam) a stone-bridge; a rock-bridge, natural bridge formed by overhanging rocks. — rdo-riṅ(s) (rdo ring(s)) a stone pillar, obelisk, as a land-mark, monument, or an ornament of buildings Glr. — rdo-ríl (rdo ril) a globular stone Pth. — rdo-léb (rdo leb) a stone slab to sit upon; or to write on etc. — rdo - sraṅ (rdo srang) a stone weight Cs. — rdo - srín (rdo srin) Glr. 50, 10, evidently a corruption of dar-srin (dar srin).

gen. *dór-je* (*dor je*) W. *dór-że* (*dor zhe*), वज्र, (Zam. also उपल) 1. precious, stone, jewel, esp. diamond, more precisely: rdó-rje p'a-lám (rdo rje pha lam); rdó-rjei gtun (rdo rje'i gtun) a knocker made of precious stones Dzl.; rdó - rjei sku (rdo rje'i sku) an adamantine body Pth.; rdó-rjei tse (rdo rje'i tshe) an adamantine life Glr.; zag-med-rdó-rje-lta-bui tsé-la mṅa brnyéd-pas (zag med rdo rje lta bu'i tshe la mnga' brnyed pas) Pth. as much as immortality; rdó-rjei ○jim-pa (rdo rje'i 'jim pa), or rín-po-čei ○jim-pa (rin po che'i 'jim pa) Glr. mortar composed of pulverized precious stones and water, and considered a cement of marvelous properties. — 2. thunderbolt, originally the weapon of Indra, with the northern Buddhists the ritual sceptre of the priests (v. Köpp. II, 271; Was. 193), held by them during their prayers in their hands and moved about in various directions; symbol of hardness and durability, also of power; source of many phantastic ideas and practices; frq. forming part of names. — 3. euphem. for p'o-rtágs (pho rtags) C. Comp. rdo-rje-gliṅ (rdo rje gling) seems to be the popular spelling of the Sanitarium in British Sikkim, which by the English generally is written Darjeeling. (Here Csoma died, and Dr Hooker staid here for some time.) Acc. to several titles of books in the Petersb. list of manuscripts, it ought properly to be spelled dar-rgyas-gliṅ (dar rgyas gling). -- rdo-rje-rgya-grám (rdo rje rgya gram) v. rgya (rgya) comp. — rdo-rje-gčód-pa (rdo rje gcod pa), वज्रच्छेदिका, title of a religious book most extensively used among Buddhists; Was. (145), Burn. I, 465. — rdo-rje-○čáṅ (rdo rje 'chang), वज्रपाणि, less frq. ○dzin ('dzin),
